primary_diagnosis.name: Severe plaque psoriasis flare with systemic burden (pustular/erythrodermic transformation and secondary infection to be excluded)

pathophysiology_summary: Plaque psoriasis is an immune-mediated disease of keratinocyte hyperproliferation and inflammation; a severe flare (often triggered by stress and loss of topical therapy) causes rapidly expanding thick plaques that fissure and bleed, with a systemic inflammatory burden producing profound fatigue. This mimics generalized pustular psoriasis, erythroderma, superimposed cellulitis/sepsis and eczema herpeticum. Reassuring features are absence of widespread sterile pustules/pus lakes, no high fever/rigors, no single spreading unilateral hot area/honey crusting/punched-out vesicles, and no temperature/haemodynamic instability. Telehealth cannot examine the full skin surface or assess secondary infection, but restarting the patient's usual high-potency topical steroid plus emollients and cooling is an appropriate remote step, alongside prompt in-person/dermatology review, skin-care to reduce infection risk, and emergency safety-netting for pustular psoriasis, erythroderma instability or secondary sepsis.

differential.evidence_basis: Extensive fissuring plaques with fatigue on a known background after stopping topical steroids.
Screen for sterile pustules and high fever.
Screen for a spreading hot area/honey crusting/systemic features.
No pustules/high fever, no spreading hot area/vesicles, no instability — a severe plaque flare.
Lower probability but must remain safety-netted (emergency if it appears).
Open fissures raise secondary-infection risk; monitor.

differential.diagnosis: Severe plaque psoriasis flare — transformation/infection safety-netted
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: restart topical steroid + emollients, prompt in-person/dermatology review and systemic-therapy planning, with emergency safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Sudden widespread sterile pustules/pus lakes with high fever/rigors (generalized pustular psoriasis/sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Generalized pustular psoriasis is a dermatological emergency with systemic risk.

red_flags.description: A single rapidly spreading unilateral hot area, honey-coloured crusting, or clustered punched-out vesicles (cellulitis/impetiginisation/eczema herpeticum)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary bacterial/viral infection can spread rapidly in fissured skin.

red_flags.description: Temperature/haemodynamic instability or feeling systemically very unwell (erythroderma/sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extensive skin failure can impair thermoregulation/fluid balance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Re-issuing topical steroid without screening/safety-netting pustular psoriasis or erythroderma.
Not screening the fissured skin for cellulitis/impetiginisation/eczema herpeticum.
Arranging IM NSAID over chat or presenting diets/supplements as therapy.
Not arranging dermatology review/systemic therapy for extensive disease.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No pustules/high fever/spreading hot area/instability — a severe but non-emergent plaque flare
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports restarting topical therapy with prompt in-person/derm review and emergency safety-netting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Full skin-surface examination and assessment of secondary infection
Objective observations if systemic features develop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen pustular/erythroderma/infection red flags; restart the usual topical steroid plus emollients/cooling and skin care; arrange prompt in-person/dermatology review and systemic-therapy planning; escalate to ED for pustular eruption, high fever/rigors, spreading infection or instability.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Re-issuing steroid without safety-netting could miss pustular psoriasis, erythroderma or secondary sepsis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Severe/extensive psoriasis warrants topical optimisation and dermatology referral for systemic/biologic therapy; pustular and erythrodermic psoriasis are emergencies.
Watch for secondary infection in fissured skin and for systemic instability requiring hospital care.
